title: Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless-type Transition in Site Percolation on the Diamond Hierarchical Lattice
authors: Takehisa Hasegawa, Kazuki Wataya, Tomoaki Nogawa
abstract: We study site percolation on the diamond hierarchical lattice, a finite-dimensional fractal network, using an exact generating-function analysis. In contrast to bond percolation, site percolation on this lattice does not undergo a transition from a nonpercolating phase to a percolating phase. Instead, the system exhibits a nonpercolating phase for $p<p_{\rm c}$ and a critical phase for $p>p_{\rm c}$. In the critical phase, the size of the largest cluster remains subextensive, scaling as $N^{ψ(p)}$, where the fractal exponent $ψ(p)$ varies continuously with $p$. By analyzing the renormalization-group recursion relation in the vicinity of $p_{\rm c}$, we show that the correlation length exhibits a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless-type essential singularity, $ξ(p)\sim \exp \left({\rm const}/\sqrt{p_{\rm c}-p}\right)$ for $p \to p_{\rm c}^-$, which is further confirmed by finite-size scaling analyses showing excellent data collapse. These results demonstrate that critical phases in percolation can emerge even on finite-dimensional networks and that exponential volume growth is not necessary for such phases to appear. We argue that the critical phase on the diamond hierarchical lattice stems from site dilution remaining relevant under renormalization.
summary: This paper demonstrates that site percolation on a diamond hierarchical lattice undergoes a BKT-type transition into a critical phase that lacks a giant cluster. It proves that exponential volume growth is not a prerequisite for critical phases, as the hierarchical structure and site dilution create persistent connectivity bottlenecks.
main_problem: The study investigates whether exponential volume growth is necessary for the emergence of critical phases in percolation and explores the nature of the phase transition in site percolation on a finite-dimensional fractal network.
main_result: The authors discover a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless (BKT)-type transition characterized by an essential singularity in the correlation length, where a critical phase exists without a giant percolating cluster.
method: The research employs exact generating-function analysis, renormalization-group (RG) recursion relations, and finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: The system is site percolation on a Diamond Hierarchical Lattice (DHL), a finite-dimensional fractal network with fractal dimension d_f = 2 constructed via a recursive replacement rule.
key_observables: Conditional contact probability (P_n), correlation length (xi), fractal exponent (psi), susceptibility (chi), and cluster-size distribution.
important_parameters: Occupation probability (p), critical threshold (p_c = 27/32), and the generation number (n) of the lattice.
main_assumptions: The derivation of scaling relations assumes that degree-2 nodes provide the dominant contribution to the root-cluster mass and that the correlation function follows a power-law decay.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the recursive construction of the DHL; Figure 2 demonstrates the sharpening of contact probability at p_c and the square-root singularity; Figure 4-6 show scaling of correlation functions, susceptibility, and data collapse via FSS; Figure 7 shows cumulative cluster-size distribution scaling.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the lattice construction and model definition, followed by the derivation of exact generating functions and RG recursion relations, an analysis of the phase transition and critical exponents, and concludes with finite-size scaling validation and discussion of the physical mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Construction of the DHL. (a) The graph Gn+1 is obtained by merging the roots of four copies of Gn as shown here. Two of the merged nodes become the roots of Gn+1, denoted by vA and vB. (b) Example of the fourth-generation graph G4.

FIG. 2. (a) Conditional contact probability Pn as a function of the occupation probability p for site percolation on the DHL. The thin red, green, and blue curves correspond to generations n = 8, 9, and 10, respectively. The dotted, dashed, dot-dashed, and solid curves correspond to the larger generations n = 20, 40, 80, and 160. The lines show analytical results obtained from the recursion relations, while the symbols represent Monte Carlo simulation data. As the generation n increases, Pn(p) becomes progressively sharper and, in the limit n →∞, exhibits a finite jump at the critical point pc (vertical dotted line), followed by a continuous increase for p > pc. (b) Log-log plot of the...

FIG. 3. (a) Normalized mean root-cluster size ¯srt n (p)/Nn and (b) fractal exponent ψn(p) as functions of the occupation probability p for site percolation on the DHL. The thin red, green, and blue curves correspond to generations n = 8, 9, and 10, respectively. The dotted, dashed, dot-dashed, and solid curves correspond to the larger generations n = 20, 40, 80, and 160. The lines show analytical results obtained from the recursion relations, while the symbols represent Monte Carlo simulation data. In panel (a), the growth of ¯srt n (p)/Nn becomes increasingly suppressed over the entire range of p as the generation n increases. Inset of panel (a): normalized mean root-cluster size ¯srt n...

FIG. 4. (a) Log-log plot of the correlation function C(r) as a function of the distance r from the root vA for site percolation on the DHL. Here, C(r) denotes the probability that a node at distance r from the root vA belongs to the root cluster. Monte Carlo simulation data obtained from 216 samples are shown, with different symbols representing different values of p. Blue, green, red, and black symbols correspond to generations n = 10, 11, 12, and 13, respectively. For p ≥pc, the data are consistent with a power-law decay over a broad range of distances. The solid lines are guides to the eye indicating the form C(r) ∼r−2[1−ψ(p)]. (b) Cumulative correlation function P

FIG. 5. Susceptibility χn(p) as a function of p for site percolation on the DHL. The thin solid, dotted, dashed, dot-dashed, and thick solid lines correspond to generations n = 10, 20, 40, 80, and 160, respectively. The lines are obtained from the recursion relations. The susceptibility exhibits a pronounced maximum below the critical point pc. As the generation n increases, the peak height grows and its position shifts toward pc, while χn(p) remains large throughout the critical phase for p > pc. Inset: comparison between the results obtained from the recursion relations (lines) and Monte Carlo simulation data (symbols) for smaller generations n = 8, 9, and 10, showing excellent agreement...
